title: Why the Ideal Case Is Rarely Reached
concept: Real-world GAN training almost never settles into that clean 50/50 balance point — it tends to overshoot, stall, or tip lopsided instead, which sets up the training struggles covered next.

exercises.prompt: Recap: In the theoretical ideal case, what happens to the discriminator's accuracy?

options.text: It settles near 50%, no better than random guessing, because the fakes are indistinguishable from real data

exercises.explain: Right — that clean 50/50 finish line is the theoretical ideal. Today: why real training almost never lands there so neatly.

cards.title: A clean finish line — on paper
cards.body: Last lesson's 50/50 balance point sounds like a tidy finish line. But if you've ever learned to ride a bike, you know that reaching 'smooth and balanced' and actually staying there right away are two very different things.

cards.title: Theory vs. real training
cards.body: The 50/50 balance point describes an ideal destination, not a guaranteed outcome. In real-world training, the generator and discriminator rarely settle into that clean, steady balance — instead, one side often surges ahead, stalls out, or the whole process overshoots back and forth.

cards.title: Wobbling before it's smooth
cards.body: Picture learning to ride a bike. In theory, perfect balance is possible — you just need the right amount of lean and pedal speed. In practice, you wobble, overcorrect, swerve too far the other way, and sometimes tip over before ever gliding smoothly. Real GAN training often wobbles the same way on its way toward (or instead of) that clean balance point.

cards.title: When one side suddenly takes over
cards.body: Instead of a smooth slide toward 50%, a real discriminator's accuracy might suddenly crash toward 0% because the generator found one trick that fools it every single time — or the discriminator might snap back to 99% and completely overpower the generator. Neither outcome is the calm, steady balance from theory.

cards.title: The math guarantees the destination, not the trip
cards.body: You might think that because the ideal balance point exists mathematically, training will naturally arrive there on its own. In reality, the formula describes where a perfect solution WOULD sit — it doesn't guarantee the step-by-step training process will actually navigate its way there.

cards.title: Theory vs. practice
cards.body: What the two paths tend to look like:

left.text:
Smooth, steady approach
Settles right at 50/50
Balance holds

right.text:
Swings and overshoots
One side may dominate
Balance is hard to hold

exercises.prompt: Why does real GAN training rarely reach the clean 50/50 balance point?

options.text: The training process can overshoot, stall, or let one side dominate instead of settling smoothly
Because discriminators are not allowed to reach 50% accuracy
Because generators only get one round of training
Because the balance point doesn't actually exist mathematically

exercises.explain: The ideal point exists on paper, but the step-by-step training process often overshoots or gets stuck instead of gliding smoothly into it.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Because the 50/50 balance point exists mathematically, real training is guaranteed to reach it.
exercises.explain: False. The math describes an ideal destination — it doesn't guarantee that real, step-by-step training will actually arrive there.

exercises.sentence: In practice, GAN training often ___ past the balance point instead of settling smoothly, like a beginner wobbling and overcorrecting on a bike.
exercises.explain: Real training tends to swing past the balance point rather than sliding gently into it.

exercises.prompt: Which scenario shows real-world training missing the clean ideal balance?

options.text: A discriminator's accuracy suddenly crashes toward 0% because the generator found one trick that fools it every time
A discriminator's accuracy gradually and smoothly settles at exactly 50% and stays there
Both networks stop training at the exact same moment by mutual agreement

exercises.explain: A sudden crash — one side completely dominating — is a classic sign of real training missing the smooth, ideal balance.

exercises.prompt: You've now seen the ideal 50/50 balance point AND why real training usually misses it. What's the natural next question to dig into?

options.text: What specifically makes GANs so hard to train in practice — the exact struggles behind that missed balance
How to draw more pixels per image
How to sort emails into folders
How to convert Celsius to Fahrenheit

exercises.explain: Exactly! Up next, Unit 205: Why GANs Are Hard to Train — we'll dig into the specific struggles behind this missed balance point.
